name: CurveBench
description: CurveBench (also referred to as CurveBench-Hard) is the hard evaluation split of the CurveBench benchmark. It contains 456 hand-drawn images across four categories — Polygon (199), Topographical (100), Maze (100), and Counting (57) — each paired with a rooted-tree annotation encoding the nested containment relationships between regions. Together with the companion CurveBench-Easy dataset (300 examples), the full CurveBench benchmark totals 756 images.
rai:dataCollection: Images were manually authored or procedurally inspired depending on the category. Polygon images use piecewise-linear drawings. Topographical images mimic elevation level-set patterns. Maze images were constructed to maximize spatial entanglement. Counting images pack high volumes of nested curves. Ground-truth rooted trees were generated using an automated OpenCV contour-based extraction pipeline and subsequently human-verified.
rai:dataAnnotationProtocol: Ground-truth rooted trees were generated using an automated OpenCV contour-based extraction pipeline that traces the boundary curves in each image and assembles parent-child containment relationships into a rooted tree. Annotations are stored as stringified Python lists of (parent, child) tuples.
rai:dataAnnotationAnalysis: Every annotation was individually human-verified to confirm structural correctness, catch missed or merged regions, and remove images with ambiguous contours.
rai:personalSensitiveInformation: The dataset does not contain personal data, faces, biometric information, private text, or human-subject records. All images are synthetic or stylized diagrams.
rai:dataBiases: Hand-authored examples reflect the drawing styles and visual conventions of the annotators. Topographical images are stylized representations, not actual geographic data. The dataset may not represent all diagrammatic, cartographic, cultural, or real-world visual styles.
rai:dataLimitations: The dataset evaluates rooted region-containment topology only. It does not measure metric geometry, 3D structure, temporal dynamics, or real-world cartographic reasoning. The Counting category has only 57 examples due to annotation complexity.
rai:dataUseCases: Research evaluation of visual topological reasoning across diverse image types; verifiable RLVR training for vision-language models; stress-testing model scalability with high-density curve configurations.
rai:dataSocialImpact: Methods trained on this dataset could contribute to automated map vectorization or geospatial analysis systems. Researchers should be aware of potential surveillance or military downstream applications.
prov:wasGeneratedBy: Data collection: Images were manually authored by the research team between 2024-09 and 2025-01 using digital drawing software. Polygon images use piecewise-linear drawings; Topographical images are stylised renderings inspired by (but not copied from) real-world topographic maps; Maze images were constructed to maximise spatial entanglement; Counting images were packed with high-volume nested curves to stress-test scaling. No third-party copyrighted imagery was used. Preprocessing: Each PNG was kept at native author resolution with white-background normalisation; no augmentation or filtering was applied. Data annotation: Ground-truth rooted trees were produced by an automated OpenCV contour-based extraction pipeline that traces boundary curves and assembles parent-child containment relationships, stored as stringified Python lists of (parent, child) tuples with node 0 fixed as the outermost (background) region. Quality control: Every annotation was independently human-verified by the research team to confirm structural correctness; ambiguous or noisy contours were corrected or removed (no formal multi-annotator agreement score was computed because annotation was algorithmic and verified, not crowd-sourced). Tools and platforms used: digital drawing software for image creation, OpenCV (Python) for contour extraction, NetworkX for tree validation, and Hugging Face Datasets for hosting and distribution.

distribution.name: repo
distribution.description: The CurveBench (Hard) Hugging Face git repository. The sha256 field is the canonical Croissant placeholder for git-backed repositories per https://github.com/mlcommons/croissant/issues/80, since a git tree does not have a single content hash.

distribution.name: parquet-files-for-config-default
distribution.description: Auto-converted Parquet files for the default config of CurveBench (Hard) on the refs/convert/parquet ref. One file per split.

recordSet.name: hard-examples
recordSet.description: All 456 CurveBench hard examples with image, category, filename, node count, and rooted-tree edge list.

field.name: image
field.description: Hand-drawn PNG image showing disjoint closed curves representing nested regions. The outermost (background) region is always node 0 in the annotation.

field.name: category
field.description: Category of the image. One of: 'Counting', 'Maze', 'Polygon', 'Topographical'.

field.name: filename
field.description: Original filename of the source image (e.g. '1.PNG'). Useful for tracking individual examples.

field.name: num_nodes
field.description: Total number of nodes in the rooted tree, including the implicit root (node 0 = outermost/background region). Equals the number of closed regions plus one.

field.name: tree
field.description: Stringified edge list of the rooted tree as a Python list of (parent, child) integer tuples. Node 0 is always the outermost region. Example: '[(0, 1), (0, 2), (1, 3)]'. Parse with ast.literal_eval().

recordSet.name: category-splits
recordSet.description: Split definitions for CurveBench (Hard). Each category is a separate HuggingFace split; a 'combined' split contains all 456 examples.

field.name: category
field.description: Category label associated with this split, or 'all' for the combined split.

field.name: size
field.description: Number of examples in this split.
